posts.caption:
On New Year’s Day in 2022, photographer @marietomanova pledged to take a self-portrait with an instant camera every day for a year. It would be a year of epic travelling for the Czech-born, New York City-based artist, as she moved between her home in NYC and Dublin, Lisbon, Czech Republic, Rome, Florence, Venice, Maine, Tokyo, Paris and Antwerp. But she didn’t anticipate quite how confronting the task ahead would be. And setting up a tripod “in the wild” wasn’t always easy. ⁠
⁠
“This project took a lot out of me,” Tomanova tells @ohnoitsemilyd. “It made me look at myself, but in ways that I didn’t even expect.” While she did make it through to 2023, in July, the photographer missed a day. “I was devastated, and then one day became two. Ultimately, I had to start again.” But this break in the continuity of the project, titled Three Empty Weeks in July, would become one of its defining features. The photo series is on display at Harkawik in New York until 18 July. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Photography by Marie Tomanova. Courtesy of the artist⁠
posts.alt_text:
@pharrell’s @louisvuitton from its inception has been based on the dandy – dressing to impress in somewhat tricksy, incredibly refined and stratospherically expensive finery – this season updated with Vuitton-branded surfing paraphernalia.⁠

Read @alexanderfury’s report at the link in bio 📲⁠

posts.caption:
For Italian fashion photographer Paolo Roversi, doubt is the open door to creativity and imagination. “I think the doubts have been growing throughout my life as a photographer …” @roversi tells @sofia_hallstrom_. “Not fewer doubts, but more and more. The more I know, the more doubts I have.”⁠
⁠
Shooting on an 8x10 Polaroid film camera, Roversi creates imagery with a luminosity that draws the eye to both the clothing and the model. The resulting photographs transport fashion into an otherworldly realm. A new major exhibition now open at @themopfoundation in A Corũna, titled Doubts, explores four decades of the photographer’s work, placing memorable masterpieces alongside previously unseen images that have spent years, as he puts it, “sleeping in boxes”. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸1. Doubts, Paris, 2026. © Paolo Roversi. Courtesy of the artist⁠
3. Molly, Paris, 2015. © Paolo Roversi. Courtesy of the artist⁠
4. Olaf, Paris, 2005. © Paolo Roversi. Courtesy of the artist⁠
6. Sara Grace, Paris, 2018. © Paolo Roversi. Courtesy of the artist⁠

posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.
Talent Westislonely @westisl0nely
Photography Jack Lee @jacklee.official

posts.caption:
Every summer, wearing flip flops isn’t a choice—it’s a must. But if, like me, you‘re tired of the same old pair, toe rings might be the easiest way to refresh your summer style.
@Havaianas and @TWOJEYS have reunited to launch three limited-edition toe rings, designed to be worn on their own or styled with your flip flops. From floral designs to colorful stone styles, each piece is simple, bold, and effortlessly chic.
To celebrate the collaboration, the brands also introduced a bespoke studded Havaianas Urban Basic flip flop and a chain TOP. Created as exclusive art pieces, they reinterpret the classic Havaianas silhouette with bold silver studs and handcrafted details, showing how toe rings can transform an everyday essential. This summer, why not style your flip flops with the Havaianas × TWOJEYS toe ring collection? The pieces are now available on the TWOJEYS website and in stores.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @havaianaseurope
#Havaianas #TwoJeys
#FlipFlops #ToeRings #SummerStyling

posts.caption:
Not even a London heatwave can stop our love for Wimbledon. You just need the right towel.
Today we stopped by Chelsea Barracks, where @christyhome, the Official @wimbledon towel supplier since 1987, and @babolat are bringing The Championships spirit into the city with a two week pop up and live screening.
Tucked into a quiet corner of Chelsea Barracks, the courtyard was transformed into a Wimbledon style lawn, with green deckchairs, parasols, mini tennis nets and plenty of summer energy.
We also got to listen to a conversation with British tennis player @norriee, who shared his own Wimbledon story and what it means to step onto the grass during one of the most iconic moments of the tennis season.
Inside, guests can shop the Official Wimbledon Collection, personalise selected pieces, try mini tennis courts with Babolat coaches, enter prizes and watch Wimbledon on the big screen. This year’s Official Championship Towel arrives in a strawberry-inspired colourway, alongside the So Last Season collection, where previous towels are reworked into tote bags, pouches and everyday accessories.
📌 Christy x Babolat Wimbledon Pop-up
• Date: 29 June to 12 July
• Location: Garrison Square, SW1W 8BG
Words by Josephine Jiang
Edited by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @a2z_fashionmag
#Wimbledon #ChristyHome
#LondonThingsToDo #LondonPopup

posts.caption:
@mercedesbenz and @kidsuper are bringing football, cars and fashion onto the same pitch.
For their latest collaboration, the two brands link seven football nations, including Brazil, Uruguay, Germany, Argentina, France, England and Spain, with iconic Mercedes-Benz vehicles from the brand’s 140-year archive. Each car represents a defining year in that country’s football history, turning sporting memory into automotive storytelling.
But this is not just about cars. Each country and vehicle pairing is reimagined through an exclusive KidSuper piece, where football kits, tailoring, patches, embroidery and bespoke details come together in Colm Dillane’s playful visual language.
The collaboration debuted during KidSuper’s Spring/Summer 2027 show in Miami, marking the brand’s first main collection show outside Paris. The limited edition Mercedes-Benz x KidSuper capsule will launch in autumn 2026, bringing together tailored menswear, leisurewear and accessories.
Swipe through and tell us your favourite pairing.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷Mercedes-Benz, KidSuper
#mercedesbenz #football #worldcup #kidsuper

posts.caption:
“Sometimes I love to overdo everything and go crazy. I love bright colours: pink, orange, sunset colours, or the colours of beautiful flowers,” says @inkawilliams as a self-described minimalist, with maximalist tendencies. The French-Australian model is a vision of effortless beauty as she embodies the youthful spirit of the Chanel Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss, its frosted finishes, vivid colours and high-shine, hypnotising tubes.
Through the link in bio, see the beauty behind Inka Williams’ salty skin, flushed cheeks and reflective lips.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.
Words: @itsalexjduffy
Photography: @jamie_.green
Makeup: @victoriabaron using @chanel.beauty
Hair: @darrensummors
Styling: @rachelwayman
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Production director: @rebeccamooreproductions
Art director: @_cathryn.s.z
Digital director: @ariellekatos
posts.alt_text:
While Bali is ingrained in @inkawilliams’ life and sense of self, the beach is something she had to leave to love. “My mum used to force us to go to the beach. I remember hating it. I’m a Virgo . . . all the sand on my hands and my feet when I would go back to the car,” she tells BAZAAR. “Eventually, when I grew up and moved to London, I realised how much I need the beach. I don’t mind if it’s cold, but I need the sun wherever I am. I need nature. If I go too long without being in nature, I start to lose my mind.”

At the link in bio, Inka Williams details her connection to Bali and the daily rituals which keep her grounded far from home. 

#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand. 

Words: @itsalexjduffy
Videography: @jordansimpsonmotion
Makeup: @victoriabaron using @chanel.beauty
Hair: @darrensummors
Styling: @rachelwayman
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Production director: @rebeccamooreproductions
Digital director: @ariellekatos

posts.caption:
When home is somewhere between the island of Bali, Melbourne, Los Angeles, London and Paris, ‘home’ is a feeling @inkawilliams finds within herself. The Australian-born model was raised in Bali, where her childhood home remains an oasis from bustling city streets and long days on set. “I just love being at home,” says Williams. “The garden is just so beautiful. I feel like I’m living in a painting. Everything [in Bali] has developed around the house, but my house is still a bubble of green. I never want to leave home.”
Through the link in bio, the model and creative multihyphenate shares more on feeling at home across the world and with her love.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.
Words: @itsalexjduffy
Photography: @jamie_.green

I don’t mind if it’s cold, but I need the sun wherever I am. I need nature. If I go too long without being in nature, I start to lose my mind.”

At the link in bio, Inka Williams details her connection to Bali and the daily rituals which keep her grounded far from home. 

#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)

Make-up Artist: Itika Chugh (@fashionmakeupbyitikachugh)
Hair Artist: Umang Artist (@umang.artist), at Anima Creatives (@animacreatives)
Style Assistant: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Maye Musk is wearing the Ethereal top with an abstract coral reef–inspired belt with sculptural organic textures, by Richa Khemka (@richakhemkalabel)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #MayeMusk

From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)

posts.caption:
A bold shift is underway in Bollywood’s blockbuster imagination. With Alia Bhatt (@aliaabhatt) and Sharvari (@sharvari) leading ‘Alpha’, the spy universe enters a new phase where scale, action, and spectacle are no longer defined by male heroes alone. The film signals a change in what mainstream entertainment can look like when women step into roles built for pure cinematic power. More than a genre experiment, it points to a widening space in commercial cinema where female stars are no longer exceptions, but central to the action itself.
Tap the link in bio to read on.

posts.caption:
On the final evening of The Business of Beauty Global Forum 2026, attendees  gathered for a celebratory dinner to honour this year’s cohort of The Business of Beauty Global Awards, selected from among more than 100 applicants from 27 countries.
In the Emerging Track, UK haircare brand Wavy (@feelwavy), founded by brothers Ariel and Eyal Booker, received the Creative Execution Award, while Indian clean fragrance brand Secret Alchemist (@secret.alchemist) was awarded the Business Innovation Award.
In the Breakthrough Track, Mexican clean beauty brand Sarelly (@sarellybeauty) took home the Creative Execution award while Dr. Diamond’s Metacine (@drdiamondsmetacine), the eponymous skincare label of American facial plastic surgeon Dr. Jason Diamond and Dr. Jessica Combs received the Business Innovation Award.
In the Established Track, Korean fragrance brand Borntostandout (@borntostandout.official), was recognised with the Creative Execution Award and UK oral care brand Suri received the Business Innovation Award.
The winners were selected by a jury that included Priya Venkatesh, chief merchandising officer at @Sephora; Scott Friedman, chief executive of Rare Beauty; @MonaKattan, founder and CEO of @Kayali; @renaudsalmon, chief creative officer at @amouageofficial; and @Danessa_Myricks, makeup artist and founder of @DanessaMyricksBeauty, alongside The Business of Fashion’s editor-in-chief and CEO @ImranAmed, and @PriyaRao, executive editor of The Business of Beauty.
Read more about the evening’s winners and the ideas shaping beauty’s next chapter #linkinbio
#BoFBeauty
posts.alt_text:
“Luxury aesthetics today means restraint,” said @drjoe_thedemis, a Thai dermatologist and co-founder of The Demis Clinic, in a talk on the latest trends and most popular procedures in plastic surgery. “Today, the true luxury is much more subtle.”

Read more on session 1 of The Business of Beauty Global Forum 2026 #linkinbio

#BoFBeauty

posts.caption:
Speaking onstage at The Business of Beauty Global Forum, @Sephora’s chief executive Guillaume Motte shared how one of the most influential retailers differentiates its offering and what its executives are looking for when hunting for new brands.
Its winning formula has helped the speciality retailer increase its sales by $5 billion in the past three years, Motte told The Business of Beauty executive editor @PriyaRao, adding that the LVMH-owned beauty retail giant has grown for the last 22 consecutive quarters.
Learn more about the strategies that have led the retailer’s growth at the #linkinbio
#BoFBeauty

posts.caption:
For more than a decade, activewear shoppers largely looked to Lululemon and Nike. But as the post-pandemic boom cools and growth becomes harder to find, a new crop of brands is gaining traction.
Smaller labels like Set Active, 437 and Oner Active aren’t reinventing activewear. They’re winning customers through social media, creator-led marketing and a deep understanding of today’s fitness culture where consumers move fluidly through workouts like pilates, Hyrox and tennis on any given week.
In this episode of The Debrief podcast, retail editor Cathaleen Chen (@kittykewlcat) joins senior correspondent Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) to discuss why these newer brands are resonating, whether their momentum is sustainable, and what their success reveals about the challenges facing industry leaders Nike and Lululemon.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 Set Active
posts.alt_text:
On the final evening of The Business of Beauty Global Forum 2026, attendees  gathered for a celebratory dinner to honour this year’s cohort of The Business of Beauty Global Awards, selected from among more than 100 applicants from 27 countries.

In the Emerging Track, UK haircare brand Wavy (@feelwavy), founded by brothers Ariel and Eyal Booker, received the Creative Execution Award, while Indian clean fragrance brand Secret Alchemist (@secret.alchemist) was awarded the Business Innovation Award.

In the Breakthrough Track, Mexican clean beauty brand Sarelly (@sarellybeauty) took home the Creative Execution award while Dr. Diamond’s Metacine (@drdiamondsmetacine), the eponymous skincare label of American facial plastic surgeon Dr. Jason Diamond and Dr. Jessica Combs received the Business Innovation Award.

In the Established Track, Korean fragrance brand Borntostandout (@borntostandout.official), was recognised with the Creative Execution Award and UK oral care brand Suri received the Business Innovation Award.

The winners were selected by a jury that included Priya Venkatesh, chief merchandising officer at @Sephora; Scott Friedman, chief executive of Rare Beauty; @MonaKattan, founder and CEO of @Kayali; @renaudsalmon, chief creative officer at @amouageofficial; and @Danessa_Myricks, makeup artist and founder of @DanessaMyricksBeauty, alongside The Business of Fashion’s editor-in-chief and CEO @ImranAmed, and @PriyaRao, executive editor of The Business of Beauty.

Read more about the evening’s winners and the ideas shaping beauty’s next chapter #linkinbio

#BoFBeauty

posts.caption:
At the height of her signature nap dress’ viral success in 2020, Hill House founder Nell Diamond (@nelliediamond) encountered an uncomfortable question from a journalist: What happens when the hype inevitably fades?
“I remember thinking ‘Wait, what do you know?’” Diamond told BoF. But she didn’t panic. Tuning out the scepticism, Diamond focused on her offering one product at a time. Keeping up momentum was always going to be tough. Other pandemic-era success stories like Entireworld and Richer Poorer faltered as lockdowns ended. Hill House, however, kept getting bigger.
BoF's Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) explores how the brand leveraged the viral success of its nap dress to build a diversified business that hit $110 million in sales last year. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @hillhouse

posts.caption:
A trained ballet and contemporary dancer, our latest cover star Sanya Malhotra (@sanyamalhotra_) is an “unconventional” actor who pirouetted her way into hearts with numerous relatable roles—and many a viral reel—carving a unique niche for herself in the industry along the way. Brides Today gets a glimpse into her universe.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor in chief: Ruchika Mehta (@ruchikamehta05)
Interview: Rewati Rau (@rewatinair) and Shraddha Chowdhury (@shraddha.skc)

Sanya is wearing a dark fuschia bridal lehenga set, Safar Collection Osaa by Adarsh (@osaabyadarsh). All jewellery by, Khurana Jewellery House via Amigos (@khuranajewelleryhouse).
#BridesToday #SanyaMalhotra
posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio. 

Editor in chief: Ruchika Mehta (@ruchikamehta05)

Hair Artist: Madhav Trehan (@hairstylist_madhav2.0)
Makeup Artist: Niccky Rajaanie (@niccky_rajaanie)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)

#BridesTodayIndia #BhumiPednekar

Happy Birthday to the forever icon, Karisma Kapoor (@therealkarismakapoor). Here's to the woman who inspired generations and continues to steal the spotlight.

posts.caption: To everyone complaining… there are more ‘real’ clothes in the showroom. This is a runway fantasy, and yet still very wearable and desirable to a lot of people.

posts.caption:
yum yum yummyyyyy 😋 i cant stop obsessing over this collection!
@louisvuitton mens spring 2027 by @pharrell
posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption: I've been a Moschino fan all my life and yes I loved Jeremy's stuff and it's the majority of my collection. I liked a lot of Adrian's stuff as well but the fits and the silhouettes were all over the place, tailoring wasn't what it was, the size ranges were weird, the bags were boring and everything was WAY more expensive, the website was terrible to use. Say what you like about Jeremy but he did his own thing and brought a vision and concepts..And FUN. A lot of Adrian's stuff felt like rehashing Franco's work and motifs but with the camp and fun sucked out. Emails announcing new ranges said things like "it's Moschino but subtle". Like.....have you met Moschino buyers? We don't want subtle. And , sadly, I don't think there's a young hip audience for Moschino at the moment, not at this price point.

posts.caption:
Dario Vitale’s first—and only—runway show for @versace seemed to ask: what if fabulous clothes were not about looking “right” or “now” but about freedom — the freedom to be whoever you want and look good by your own wild, hedonistic standards?
In this week’s edition of CNN’s newsletter “Big Style” Rachel Tashjian unpacks the mystery behind Vitale’s short-lived tenure at the Italian brand and speaks to some familiar fashion faces about the power in wearing Vitale’s designs — along with why it should encourage the industry at large to finally pivot away from quiet luxury.
Are you on the list? You can get weekly emails from CNN senior style reporter and advanced outfit anthropologist @theprophetpizza, excavating stylish amusements and contextualizing the world of fashion. Sign up at the link in our bio or comment "Big Style" and we'll hook you up.
📸: c/o Versace
posts.alt_text:
If the job of a debut album is to introduce an artist to the world, while a sophomore album reinforces their reputation, then a third album offers a channel for artists to expand beyond the familiar tricks and peculiarities that shot them to fame in the first place. In Olivia Rodrigo’s case, her new album “You Seem Pretty Sad For a Girl So In Love” mostly ditches the pop punk and alt-rock references and, instead, borrows sounds that defined the ‘80s. Think New Order, or post-punk flourishes from The Cure (the English band’s frontman, Robert Smith, even makes an appearance on the album’s B-side track, “What’s Wrong with Me”). Rodrigo’s wardrobe has naturally followed suit.

From ‘60s-esque mod-inspired shift dresses, @repettoparis ballet flats, lacy @chloe bloomers, and stage-commanding platform boots, Rodrigo’s outfitting for her new “You Seem Pretty Sad” era is intentionally paying homage to a legion of influential women in music who came before her—while still authentically representing her Gen-Z POV on fashion.

“She knows how young people are dressing right now,” says stylist and Depop trends spokesperson @gabriellak_j . “She is one!”

Rodrigo’s recent wardrobe contributes to the album’s worldbuilding, but her new style hasn’t been formed in a vacuum, either. Head to the link in bio for a full analysis of the intentional curation behind the pop star’s evolving style.

📸: Getty Images

posts.caption:
In the sweltering heat, models walked the @rickowensonline menswear show at @palaisdetokyo wearing inflatable suits, jackets, lashings of leather, black and enormous boots.
The show, which was scheduled to take place in the early afternoon, was moved due to extreme heat and a red weather warning. Guests were handed water bottles from buckets of ice and those early to the event nabbed complimentary umbrellas for sun shade (in black, of course). It was a sweaty affair, on and off the runway, but models were helpfully doused in a waterwork show and spritzing fountains.
📹 📸 : CNN

posts.caption:
At the recent @milanfashionweek men’s (and the preceding @pittiuomo_official, the biannual Italian trade show for men’s clothing in Florence), dainty little details were everywhere — reminding us that styling is how you really have fun with fashion.
The riot of small-scale design flourishes seen across the Spring-Summer 2027 shows — from pins and corsages to feather boas and arm garters — means you can expect men to be more decorated than usual next summer.
📹: Courtesy Simone Rocha
📸: Courtesy Dunhill/Mirella Malaguti/WWD/Getty Images/Alessandro Garofalo/Reuters/Federico Lomartire/WWD/Getty Images/Victor Virgile/Gamma-Rapho/Jacopo Raule/Mirella Malaguti/WWD/
@simonerocha_ @dunhill @dolcegabbana @paulsmith @prada

posts.caption:
This evening, @louisvuitton built fashion week guests an oasis amidst the sweltering Parisian heat. A mighty man-made beach basked in the background of the brands’s Spring-Summer 2027 menswear collection, spritzing some much needed water into the air. Models emerged from the shoreline set to walk on a sandy runway in clothes that were business on the top, vacation on the bottom. There were blazers worn with relaxed, embellished jeans, shade-blocking baseball caps and wetsuit tops worn with corduroy trousers. One look, which included a surf-board rendered in LV’s signature checkerboard, featured a two piece suit paired with a ringer t-shirt, skater-inspired sneakers and of course long hair styled in textured, beachy coils.
🎥: CNN
📸: Simon Wohlfahrt /AFP/Getty Images

posts.alt_text:
There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

Photographer: @laaapavi
Makeup: @pircillapae (@aframe_agency)
Hair: @eduardomendezhair (@aframe_agency)
Editor-in-Chief: @faith_xue 
Senior Fashion Editor: @ella_okeeffe 
Social Creative Lead: @tashasheridan 
Social Strategy Lead: @kalaherh
Production: @gillianstern

posts.caption:
This week, London’s fashion and art scene gathered at the Serpentine Galleries in Hyde Park for the gallery’s annual summer soirée. Hosted by director @ishaambani, the night drew everyone from Alexa Chung to Rebel Wilson to Salma Hayek Karwai, all gathering to explore this year’s installations: a bold brick structure by Mexico City’s Lanza Atelier and a breathtaking fabric piece by artist Lahia Gohar. Art, architecture, and a seriously good guest list—swipe to see it all.
Photos: @huyylluong and Getty Images

posts.caption:
When The Great Gatsby premiered in 2013, audiences weren’t just watching a film, they were witnessing one of fashion’s most influential collaborations with cinema.
Working alongside Academy Award-winning costume designer Catherine Martin, Miuccia Prada reimagined more than 40 archival Prada and Miu Miu looks for Baz Luhrmann’s adaptation of F. Scott Fitzgerald’s classic. Rather than recreating the 1920s with historical precision, the costumes offered a contemporary interpretation of the Jazz Age, blending Prada’s modern sensibility with the glamour, excess, and fantasy that defined Gatsby’s world.￼
Covered in crystals, sequins, feathers, fringe, velvet, and silk, the garments were designed not only to look spectacular, but to move with the film’s sweeping cinematography. The result blurred the line between costume and couture, proving that fashion could become an essential part of cinematic storytelling rather than simply dressing its characters.
The collaboration had a lasting impact far beyond Hollywood. It reignited global fascination with the Roaring Twenties, fueling a wave of Art Deco-inspired fashion, embellished eveningwear, and Gatsby-themed events throughout the 2010s. At the same time, it reinforced the growing relationship between luxury fashion houses and cinema, showing that archival fashion could be reimagined as living cultural history instead of remaining confined to museums or runways.
Following the film’s release, Prada: The Great Gatsby costume exhibitions opened in New York, Tokyo, and later Shanghai, allowing the public to experience the designs as works of art in their own right. Their legacy continued years later, when several original dresses, including the now-iconic chandelier dress, were pulled from the archive for Dua Lipa’s 2021 We’re Good music video, proving that their influence extended well beyond the silver screen.
Film: The Great Gatsby
Director: Baz Luhrmann
Costume Design: Catherine Martin
Fashion Collaboration: Miuccia Prada
Fashion Houses: Prada, Miu Miu
Lead Cast: Carey Mulligan, Leonardo DiCaprio, Tobey Maguire, Joel Edgerton
posts.alt_text:
Long before street style became dominated by fashion weeks, luxury logos, and carefully curated content, Hel Looks was documenting something far more interesting: real people.

Founded in 2005 by Liisa Jokinen and Sampo Karjalainen, the Helsinki-based project has spent over two decades capturing the city’s unique approach to personal style. Rather than focusing on trends, Hel Looks celebrates individuality, people who dress for themselves, not for an audience. 

What makes Helsinki’s style so compelling is its sense of freedom. Vintage pieces sit alongside handmade garments, borrowed clothes, thrifted finds, and unexpected combinations that would feel out of place almost anywhere else. The result is a city where fashion feels less like performance and more like self-expression. 

Over the years, Hel Looks has become an archive of subcultures, creativity, and everyday originality. Each portrait is accompanied by personal reflections on clothing, inspiration, and identity, creating a portrait not only of fashion, but of the people behind it. As trends come and go, the project continues to prove that personal style will always be more interesting than following the crowd. 

In a world increasingly shaped by algorithms, Hel Looks remains a reminder that the best style often happens on ordinary streets, worn by ordinary people who never intended to become fashion references in the first place.

Credits

Project: Hel Looks
Founded: 2005
Creators & Photographers: Liisa Jokinen and Sampo Karjalainen @hellooks 
Location: Helsinki
Website: Hel Looks

posts.caption:
As a member of the Fugees and later as a solo artist, Lauryn Hill helped redefine what a female musician could look and sound like. At a time when the industry often pushed women into narrow categories, she created her own lane, effortlessly blending hip-hop, soul, R&B, and reggae while developing a visual identity that felt equally distinctive.
Oversized silhouettes, headwraps, vintage denim, tailored jackets, sportswear, bold jewelry, and natural beauty became part of her signature. Her style never felt driven by trends. Instead, it reflected confidence, individuality, and cultural pride, inspiring generations of artists who followed.
The release of The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill in 1998 cemented her place in music history. The album became a cultural landmark, earning critical acclaim while influencing countless musicians across genres. But Lauryn Hill’s impact extended beyond music. She showed that authenticity could be more powerful than image, and that personal expression could leave a deeper mark than any trend cycle.
More than two decades later, traces of her influence can still be found everywhere, from contemporary music and fashion campaigns to the resurgence of 1990s and early 2000s style. Many have borrowed from her aesthetic. Few have matched her presence.
Lauryn Hill didn’t just define an era.
She became the blueprint.

Rather than recreating the 1920s with historical precision, the costumes offered a contemporary interpretation of the Jazz Age, blending Prada’s modern sensibility with the glamour, excess, and fantasy that defined Gatsby’s world.￼

Covered in crystals, sequins, feathers, fringe, velvet, and silk, the garments were designed not only to look spectacular, but to move with the film’s sweeping cinematography. The result blurred the line between costume and couture, proving that fashion could become an essential part of cinematic storytelling rather than simply dressing its characters. 

The collaboration had a lasting impact far beyond Hollywood. It reignited global fascination with the Roaring Twenties, fueling a wave of Art Deco-inspired fashion, embellished eveningwear, and Gatsby-themed events throughout the 2010s. At the same time, it reinforced the growing relationship between luxury fashion houses and cinema, showing that archival fashion could be reimagined as living cultural history instead of remaining confined to museums or runways. 

Following the film’s release, Prada: The Great Gatsby costume exhibitions opened in New York, Tokyo, and later Shanghai, allowing the public to experience the designs as works of art in their own right. Their legacy continued years later, when several original dresses, including the now-iconic chandelier dress, were pulled from the archive for Dua Lipa’s 2021 We’re Good music video, proving that their influence extended well beyond the silver screen. 

Credits

Film: The Great Gatsby
Director: Baz Luhrmann
Costume Design: Catherine Martin
Fashion Collaboration: Miuccia Prada
Fashion Houses: Prada, Miu Miu
Lead Cast: Carey Mulligan, Leonardo DiCaprio, Tobey Maguire, Joel Edgerton

posts.caption:
Few figures have left a mark on fashion and popular culture quite like Edie Sedgwick.
With her cropped silver-blonde hair, oversized chandelier earrings, and effortless sense of style, she became one of the defining faces of 1960s New York. More than a socialite, actress, or model, Edie embodied a certain kind of magnetism that couldn’t be manufactured. She didn’t just influence fashion—she became part of its mythology.
After arriving in New York, Sedgwick quickly became a fixture of Andy Warhol’s Factory, the creative epicenter of the city’s underground art scene. Warhol recognized her charisma almost immediately, casting her in several of his films and transforming her into the unofficial star of the Factory. Together, they created an image that would define an era: part glamour, part rebellion, part beautiful chaos.
What made Edie so captivating was her ability to appear both untouchable and vulnerable at the same time. She moved through New York’s cultural circles like a shooting star, becoming a muse not only to artists and filmmakers, but to fashion itself. Decades later, her influence can still be seen everywhere—from modern It-girls and indie sleaze aesthetics to the enduring fascination with downtown cool.
Yet behind the flashbulbs and attention was a far more complicated reality. Struggling with addiction, mental health issues, and the pressures of sudden fame, Sedgwick’s life began to unravel almost as quickly as it had risen. She died in 1971 at just 28 years old, leaving behind a legacy that remains both inspiring and heartbreaking.
More than fifty years later, Edie Sedgwick continues to symbolize a particular kind of beauty—one that is imperfect, spontaneous, and impossible to replicate. Not simply an icon, but a legend whose story still haunts fashion, art, and New York itself.

posts.caption:
Between 1998 and 2002, photographer Norbert Schoerner helped redefine the visual language of the brand, creating a series of campaigns that remain some of the most distinctive in fashion history. At a time when luxury advertising often relied on glamour, fantasy, and polished perfection, Schoerner moved in the opposite direction.
His Prada was strange, clinical, and deliberately unsettling.
Working with recurring faces such as Angela Lindvall, Schoerner abandoned conventional beauty photography in favor of something more observational. Extreme close-ups, visible skin texture, awkward gestures, harsh lighting, and sterile environments transformed the campaigns into studies of human behavior rather than traditional advertisements. Models were no longer distant ideals but subjects under scrutiny.
What made the work feel so revolutionary was its embrace of imperfection. Nail biting, absent-minded expressions, dirt-covered hands, and moments that would normally be edited out became central elements of the imagery. The result captured the tension between beauty and discomfort that has long defined Prada’s identity.
Visually, the campaigns also reflected a unique moment in time. The late 1990s and early 2000s saw analogue and digital technologies collide, and Schoerner’s images embraced that transition. Rather than hiding technological imperfections, the campaigns often amplified them, creating photographs that felt futuristic, fragmented, and slightly disorienting.
More than twenty years later, the influence of this period can still be seen throughout fashion photography. The psychological distance, the artificial environments, and the fascination with unconventional beauty all became hallmarks of modern Prada imagery.
In 2024, Schoerner’s work for the house was compiled in PRADA ARCHIVE 1998–2002, a publication that reaffirmed the significance of these campaigns within the brand’s history. Looking back, they feel less like advertisements and more like pieces of contemporary art—evidence of a moment when Prada wasn’t simply selling clothes, but reshaping how fashion could be seen.

posts.caption:
Fashion after dark.
This editorial captures the chaos, glamour, and excess of nightlife.
Created by Steven Klein for the magazine’s February 2017 issue, the story unfolds like a fever dream of fashion, celebrity, and self-invention. Shot through the raw lens of Polaroid photography, the editorial blurs the line between reality and performance, transforming its cast into larger-than-life characters suspended somewhere between a downtown club, an underground art scene, and a fantasy world of their own.
What makes the editorial so compelling is its sense of unpredictability. Beauty becomes exaggerated, identities become fluid, and glamour is pushed to its most theatrical extreme. Glitter-covered faces, dramatic silhouettes, fur coats, latex, couture, and eccentric styling collide in a series of images that feel both nostalgic and futuristic at the same time.
As one of the final projects developed under Franca Sozzani’s vision for Vogue Italia, The Polaroid Issue celebrates the kind of fearless creativity that defined the magazine for decades. Rather than documenting fashion, it creates an entire universe around it—one where excess, fantasy, and individuality are not only welcomed, but celebrated.
Nearly a decade later, the editorial still feels strikingly contemporary. In an era of carefully curated perfection, its messy glamour, artistic freedom, and unapologetic weirdness feel more relevant than ever.
Editorial: The Polaroid Issue
Magazine: Vogue Italia, February 2017
Photographer: Steven Klein
Fashion Editors/Stylists: Patti Wilson, Arianne Phillips, Carlyne Cerf de Dudzeele
Hair: Ward Stegerhoek, Shay Ashual
Makeup: Sammy Mourabit, Yadim
Casting: Angus Munro, Noah Shelley
Manicure: Honey
Set Design: Jesse Kaufmann
Featured Models: Achok Majak, Anna Cleveland, Anna Ewers, Barbara Fialho, Caroline Vreeland, Christina Kruse, Coco Rocha, Crystal Renn, Dilone, Ebony Riley, Erin O’Connor, Guinevere van Seenus, Jasmine Sanders, Jordan Barrett, Li Xiao Xing, Nataša Vojnović, Stella Lucia and others.

posts.caption:
Luxury keeps changing.
Yet pearls keep surviving.
Fashion changes.
Trends change.
Consumer preferences change.
Pearls remain.
In 2026, they’re once again at the centre of some of the jewellery world’s biggest conversations.
That should make every retailer pay attention.
Because when a material remains relevant across generations, markets and design movements, it’s no longer a trend story.
It’s a luxury story.
💬 One question:
If you were building a high jewellery collection today, would pearls be essential?
Why or why not?
Image credit @jckevents
#DiamondWorld #GemSphere #Pearls #HighJewellery #JewelleryTrends
posts.alt_text:
💎 THE FUTURE OF DIAMONDS, IN THEIR WORDS

An exclusive Diamond World series featuring perspectives from the official panelists of the 41st World Diamond Congress, Singapore 🇸🇬 |12–15 July 2026.

What if the industry is asking the wrong question? 💎

For years, we’ve asked:

“How do we build natural diamond demand?”

Dave Bonaparte believes the better question is:

“How do we rebuild it?”

Because demand hasn’t disappeared.

It’s shifting.

The real opportunity now lies in understanding where consumers are moving — and how the industry can evolve alongside them.

💬 Where do you think natural diamond demand is shifting today?

#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #DiamondDemand #DaveBonaparte #DiamondIndustry

💎 THE FUTURE OF DIAMONDS, IN THEIR WORDS
An exclusive Diamond World series featuring perspectives from the official panelists of the 41st World Diamond Congress, Singapore 🇸🇬 |12–15 July 2026.
Could blockchain become the next standard for diamond ownership? 💎
Tim Goodman believes the industry is approaching a major shift — from paper certificates and PDFs to secure digital ownership powered by blockchain.
His prediction?
Within two years, every diamond sale could be recorded with a digital utility token.
As traceability, provenance and authenticity become increasingly important, the bigger question is no longer if the industry will digitise, but how quickly it will happen.
💬 Do you believe blockchain will become standard practice in the diamond industry within the next two years?
#DiamondWorld #DiamondTraceability #Blockchain #DiamondIndustry #FutureOfDiamonds

posts.caption:
Have you heard? There’s a major heatwave going on in Europe.
Wednesday was the hottest day ever recorded in France, prompting the Eiffel Tower and the Louvre to restrict visiting hours.  Schools canceled classes. The Pride parade was postponed. Almost 50 people drowned while swimming to cool off. Two children died after being left in a hot vehicle. Summer celebrating in the streets was canceled as hospitals overfilled.
And yet, Paris Menswear Fashion Week pressed on. Louis Vuitton built a working tidal wave on a fake beach. Rick Owens and Dior moved their outdoor shows to early in the morning. Many of the Spring/Summer 2027 collections featured layered sweaters, coats and jackets, some made of plastic or leather, with front row attendees in brand-supplied outfits that seemed to scoff at the weather.
Looking at the content pouring out of Paris Menswear Fashion Week alongside the news of the city’s heatwave raises some concerns: Should we be holding these events while other institutions are temporarily closing? And, looking at the broader picture, is fashion using these spaces to respond to the climate crisis knowing that the industry itself is partially responsible for it?
“The calendar does not make any sense,” Jonathan Anderson said to the Associated Press, of the way the industry operates.
But is the calendar the only problem? High fashion tends to skirt the kind of criticism that fast fashion receives for contributing to climate change, but it’s time to consider the carbon footprint left behind from weeks and weeks of fashion weeks every year. Grand ephemeral set design, loads of air travel, scores of private vehicles, and accommodations for hundreds of visitors add to emissions in cities like Paris, where AC is difficult to install due to historical architecture protected by law.
Can fashion weeks as we know them stay the same and be (literally) sustainable? Or is the heatwave laying bare the truth about them: that we need to adjust our expectations, slow down, and find more responsible ways to engage with fashion?

posts.caption:
It began when Anthony Vaccarello posted a promotional image for Saint Laurent Menswear SS27 featuring an extended-toe derby made of transparent plastic. The internet was instantly horrified by the shape, the fact that the model’s foot was visible, and the imminent sweat that would fill the shoe. Isn’t there a heatwave in France right now? The drama called to mind Chanel’s recent half-shoe debacle; Is ragebait footwear fashion’s new fetish?
Later, Madonna showed up to the front row in a fuchsia Saint Laurent mini dress. She was seated between Connor Storrie and Charli XCX, whom she promptly began smoking with (to other attendee’s chagrin.) Madonna and Storrie then had a seemingly tense conversation that has prompted Storrie stans to seek out lip readers. Do they have beef we don’t know about? If you don’t already know, Storrie’s fans are, uh, especially passionate, and track every detail of his life. But, more on that shortly.
The actual show was mostly unsurprising, leaning into Vaccarello’s trademark tailoring and juiced up 80s silhouettes. Then came Look 33: camel colored undies styled with a tan sweater and blazer, a camel choker accessory, espresso calf-socks, and the plastic derbys. Front row onlookers appeared shocked, distressed, and maybe even aroused.
But the biggest drama apparently happened before all of this, as Storrie’s superfans waited outside Hotel Le Meurice to catch a glimpse of the actor on his way to the show. Apparently, an X user who was formerly known as “clubchalamet” (now “Fan Account Storrie Glorrie”) was violently assaulted by another Storrie superfan in the crowd. In true stan fashion, it is a convoluted, verbose, and deeply ‘online’ tale that isn’t easy for outsiders to wrap their heads around. In any case some kind of physical altercation took place between the two and social media is reeling.
There is a happy ending to all this, though. And that, of course, is the Madonna-fueled Saint Laurent afterparty where she and Charli XCX danced onstage at Paradis Latin. Is a collab in the works? Hopefully it’ll be enough for us all to heal after a very hectic day of Paris Fashion Week chaos.

posts.caption:
Madonna has officially confirmed that her long-awaited, self-authored biopic was dropped due to disagreements over budgeting and film locations.
“We had a falling out, me and Universal, regarding budget because I needed—I’ve had an extraordinary life. I’ve had a huge life, so I needed a big budget,” the pop star said in a Q&A with Interview magazine published yesterday alongside a theatrical set of photos by Nadia Lee Cohen.
Universal doesn’t seem to understand who they were working with. Madonna claimed that despite the production company’s skepticism, she’d found a way to “make [the film] for less money in Serbia” but they still didn’t believe she could pull it off.
“One of their first reactions was, ‘We don’t believe you’d stay in Serbia more than four days.’ And I said, ‘Did you read the script?’ My whole life has been survival. I’m not going there for a holiday.”
Through with Universal’s antics, Madonna attempted to buy her own script back from Universal for an “extortionist’s price.”
Madonna also confirmed that she worked with Netflix on turning the idea into a series. She, a seasoned film star and cinephile, admitted that series are a different breed entirely, and that she found herself dizzied by the process. Unable to find a writer and a showrunner for the project, she turned back to music; And that is how her upcoming album ‘Confessions on a Dance Floor: Part II’ was born.
“I thought the world is in a very dark place,” Madonna said, “and people need to dance.”
Indeed it is a dark world where even Madonna can’t get a biopic made because one of the world’s top production companies is too busy throwing money at films like ‘The Super Mario Galaxy Movie.’ Nevertheless, the star went on to share the amazing story of her early career at Danceteria — a story that is highlighted in her new song of the same name, and which is also memorialized in her new short film ‘Confessions II - The Film.”
The Danceteria story is, in itself, even without ornamentation, a feature-length pitch for the ages. Maybe someone with a good head on their shoulders will hear it and get to work on the biopic. But, until that happens, who wants to go dancing?

Have you heard? There’s a major heatwave going on in Europe.

Wednesday was the hottest day ever recorded in France, prompting the Eiffel Tower and the Louvre to restrict visiting hours.  Schools canceled classes. The Pride parade was postponed. Almost 50 people drowned while swimming to cool off. Two children died after being left in a hot vehicle. Summer celebrating in the streets was canceled as hospitals overfilled. 

And yet, Paris Menswear Fashion Week pressed on. Louis Vuitton built a working tidal wave on a fake beach. Rick Owens and Dior moved their outdoor shows to early in the morning. Many of the Spring/Summer 2027 collections featured layered sweaters, coats and jackets, some made of plastic or leather, with front row attendees in brand-supplied outfits that seemed to scoff at the weather.

Looking at the content pouring out of Paris Menswear Fashion Week alongside the news of the city’s heatwave raises some concerns: Should we be holding these events while other institutions are temporarily closing? And, looking at the broader picture, is fashion using these spaces to respond to the climate crisis knowing that the industry itself is partially responsible for it?

“The calendar does not make any sense,” Jonathan Anderson said to the Associated Press, of the way the industry operates.

But is the calendar the only problem? High fashion tends to skirt the kind of criticism that fast fashion receives for contributing to climate change, but it’s time to consider the carbon footprint left behind from weeks and weeks of fashion weeks every year. Grand ephemeral set design, loads of air travel, scores of private vehicles, and accommodations for hundreds of visitors add to emissions in cities like Paris, where AC is difficult to install due to historical architecture protected by law. 

Can fashion weeks as we know them stay the same and be (literally) sustainable? Or is the heatwave laying bare the truth about them: that we need to adjust our expectations, slow down, and find more responsible ways to engage with fashion?

posts.caption:
Yesterday, Olivia Rodrigo unveiled Daisy Chain Fields Festival and the all-women lineup is giving major Lilith Fair energy. Taking over Irvine, CA this summer, the fest is stacked with heavy hitters, including Rodrigo herself alongside Bikini Kill, Chappell Roan, Doechii, Katseye, and many more. And if that wasn’t enough, the list also features special guests Karen O, Sarah McLachlan, and Stevie Nicks (!!!)
“Truly never felt more excited to share a piece of news with you all,” the singer shared on Instagram. “I’ve had a dream of doing this festival for years and i am so ecstatic its finally coming true!! Daisy Chain Fields features an all-women lineup and 100 percent of the net proceeds will go to charities dedicated to advancing and advocating for women and girls.”
According to the festival’s website, net proceeds will benefit non-profit organizations like Black Mamas Matter Alliance, Johns Hopkins Center for Indigenous Health, National Domestic Workers Alliance, Planned Parenthood, and several others.
“I firmly believe that joy, community, and music can be the drivers of meaningful change and I’m hopeful this festival will be just that,” Rodrigo added. “I absolutely cannot wait to scream and dance and sing with you guys August 29th!!!!”
#oliviarodrigo #music #singer #festival #lilithfair

posts.alt_text: This garmi is sending me to Mercury!!!

posts.caption:
Apparently the streets, the group chats, and the SPAM girilies are all saying Instagram now lets you REPLY to comments with photos from your camera roll? Excuse?
What a remarkable moment in the history of civilisation. We invented language, built the internet, AI, and have finally arrived at the ability to leave completely unsolicited images under a stranger’s comment. A blurry screenshot. A reaction meme from 2018. Your neighbour’s cat. A grainy airport paparazzi shot. The possibilities are genuinely terrifying.
Let’s test this little social experiment. If it’s real, drop your favourite WhatsApp sticker in the comments. I need to witness this technological leap with my own eyeS!!
posts.alt_text: This garmi is sending me to Mercury!!!

posts.caption:
@c.syresmith has us (and y’all, the community) on a #DSexclu tour of his @christianlouboutin exhibition. It’s giving archaeological, henny 🔴💅
It’s the classic dystopian tale. The world as we know it has been abandoned. Humanity has vanished. Civilisation has crumbled. And all that remains are RED HOT Louboutins. Foam shoes, razor-sharp patent boots, and leather quiver-and-arrow bags (hello, Katniss Everdeen) now sit like prehistoric artefacts, clues to a forgotten land and its impossibly fashionable inhabitants. Grab your fossil brushes and shovels, girlies. It’s time to start digging for those RED BOTTOMS. 🔴👀💅

Archival pull ho to aisi 💅

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
On @kirandeep_chahal: LOVE ring in white gold & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in white gold, sapphires, & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in rose gold, pink sapphires & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in yellow gold, tsavorite garnets & diamonds, all by @cartier. Mala top by @raw_mango. Miss Z Riviera heels by @christianlouboutin.

Photographer: Noémi Ottilia Szabo (@noemiottiliaszabo)

She talks about stepping back, choosing carefully, and what she no longer offers up. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the full story.
.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega

Photographer: Noémi Ottilia Szabo (@noemiottiliaszabo)
Stylist: Rhia Kapoor (@rhiakapoor)

posts.caption:
#ELLEShowTime: Every time a female superhero arrives on our screens, she carries a burden her male counterparts rarely do. She isn't simply expected to save the world; she is expected to save an entire genre, a random house, the whole world, male expectations... The list goes on. When Supergirl lands in cinemas this week, much of the conversation has already been written. "Superhero fatigue." "Another mediocre comic-book film." "Not as good as last year's Superman." Some of these criticisms are fair. Okay, let's talk about the story, the dialogues, etc. But I'd bet my money on it: others feel strangely familiar. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the complete review.
Words by: @16ektasinha
[Supergirl, DC Comics, Superman]

posts.caption:
#ELLEFashion: RIP Slouchy! Looks like skinny jeans are here to stay. At the recently concluded Milan Fashion Week, Miuccia Prada and Raf Simons repurposed skinny jeans with a creamy heft - from crafting it in buttery leather to tailoring it in clean white nylon organza and mulberry cotton. With Gucci, Dior and other houses also showing skinny pants, clearly the Y2K-era silhouette is back and is likely to dominate upcoming seasons too.
Words by: @dandydujour
#SkinnyJeans

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw
Hair: Orlando Pita at Orlo Salon @hairbyorlandopita @orlosalon
Makeup: Fulvia Farolfi at MA+ Group @fulviafarolfi @maworldgroup
Manicure: Deborah Lippmann for Deborah Lippmann @thelippmannofficial @thedeborahlippmann
Set design: Marla Weinhoff at 11th House Agency @marlaweinhoff @11thhouseagency
Produced by Alexey Galetskiy Productions @alexeyg
posts.alt_text:
Pop is in its pointe era.
 
Just look at the music video for @oliviarodrigo's “Stupid Song,” in which @nycballet ballerinas dance gorgeously around the singer in the streets of New York. Then there's @rosalia.vt, who performs in pointe shoes during the opening number of her current tour. Even rising pop star @adelajergova—a former ballerina herself—has incorporated pointe into her stage choreography. Of course, ballet and pop music have found themselves intertwined many times before, but this iteration of balletcore goes beyond adopting the aesthetics into our closets; it’s about respecting the art while embracing its exciting new forms.
 
“We finally have an opportunity to get something right in the ballet world,” says ballerina @tilerpeck. And whether these performances count as real technical ballet or not, she sees ballet's pop era as expanding its audience: “I’m all for it—especially if you have it, you know what I mean?”
 
At the link in bio, Peck talks to ELLE about how these pop stars are incorporating ballet into their performances—and how they might inspire a new generation of dancers.

Even rising pop star @adelajergova—a former ballerina herself—has incorporated pointe into her stage choreography. Of course, ballet and pop music have found themselves intertwined many times before, but this iteration of balletcore goes beyond adopting the aesthetics into our closets; it’s about respecting the art while embracing its exciting new forms.
“We finally have an opportunity to get something right in the ballet world,” says ballerina @tilerpeck. And whether these performances count as real technical ballet or not, she sees ballet's pop era as expanding its audience: “I’m all for it—especially if you have it, you know what I mean?”
At the link in bio, Peck talks to ELLE about how these pop stars are incorporating ballet into their performances—and how they might inspire a new generation of dancers.

Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.

Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”

For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
For his spring 2027 collection, @willychavarria tapped into fashion’s power to create community.
Presented today in Paris, “Comunión”—a collection title evoking both religious communion and solidarity—spoke to the sense of community that has formed around the brand. The idea was only underscored by Chavarria’s decision to show at the Espace Niemeyer, the headquarters of the French Communist Party in Paris’s 19th arrondissement, where model including Romeo Beckham, @bella_freud, and @nyknicks champion @jordanclarksons marched in a circular formation before gathering together to close the show.
“This season is about balancing tailoring with ease,” the designer tells ELLE. “Relaxed suiting, lots of shorts, and structured knits paired to create silhouettes that feel effortless, while strong shoulders and our pointed collars bring confidence and presence.”
Read our full review of the collection at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Four years ago this week, the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade. At the time, more Americans—eight in 10, in fact—supported reproductive freedom than at any other point in history. Today, 21 states have banned or restricted abortion since the Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization decision, pushing care out of reach and leading to the preventable deaths of pregnant women. More so, doctors can be arrested, jailed, and sued into ruin for providing health care. And it’s not just mothers who are dying; these bans are causing newborns to die, too.
For @phoebegates, who has learned from activists, providers, and organizers across the country about what survival looks like in post-Dobbs America, it’s become clear that the shaky legal ground, the impossible guidelines, the state-by-state chaos—none of it is accidental. Yet despite all this, she remains hopeful about the future.
“In these four years, there have also been telling signs of progress,” she writes. “Big fights in small towns have become stepping stones—win enough of them, ordinance by ordinance, city by city, and they become the path to a country that wins its reproductive freedom back. And it’s working.”
In a new essay for ELLE, Gates reflects on our post-Dobbs reality—and the extraordinary examples of resilience and innovation across the country, especially as voters get ready to vote on the matter in the November midterms. Read now at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Let’s be honest: There’s no such thing as Botox in a bottle. If you want the effects, you’ll have to get the real deal. The same can be said for most aesthetic treatments—while many very good products exist, none will rival the power of an in-office laser or biostimulatory injectable. But since some aesthetic treatments can have side effects and cost thousands of dollars, you should know which are truly top of the line before booking an appointment.
That’s why, for our third annual Tools and Treatments Awards, we recruited 28 ELLE staff members to try the most sought-after treatments. Yes, we tested the gold standards like @botoxcosmetic and @skinpen. But we also tried new and buzzy treatments, like XERF from @lutronic_us and @cynosurelasers, a radiofrequency skin tightener from Korea, which Kim Kardashian called her “new fave,” and @exomindbtl, a transcranial magnetic stimulation device that helps regulate your mood and emotions. Tap the link in bio for the best aesthetic treatments on the market.

Rosewood Hong Kong (@rosewoodhongkong) sits within this rhythm of being a traveller’s paradise. Set along the glinting edge of the historic Victoria Harbour that built Hong Kong’s two big draws—trade and tourism—Rosewood Hotel gives you the front-row seat to the city’s enduring drama. The hotel pairs the scale of its soaring 65-storey presence with local art, tactile luxury and a strong sense of place. In fact, from its Asaya Pool suspended above Victoria Harbour, the city’s skyline turns the view into pure spectacle.

posts.caption: My outfit definitely expresses how I’m feeling. I might get extra done up if I’m feeling bad or in a funk and I don’t feel good when I’m not put together. Also I love style and fashion it’s like art for me putting outfits together. But there are many people that just get dressed and don’t think about it one way or another so I’m not sure that it affects everyone in the same way.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

For over six decades, Femina Miss India has been more than a pageant. It’s woven into the cultural fabric of India. It is a platform that has consistently celebrated ambition, individuality, resilience, and purpose, highlighting the inherent beauty of our women on the global stage.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
As this #Pride month comes to a close, we’re reflecting on how inclusivity and sustainability work together 💚⁠
⁠
Charlie McEvoy, founder of @plantfacedclothing, knows this very well—and has folded this into their approach to running their brand via gender neutrality. “Gender neutral clothing and sustainability are closely connected because both encourage a more thoughtful approach to consumption,” McEvoy explained. “When garments are designed to be worn by anyone, they often have a broader appeal and a longer lifespan. They can be shared between partners, friends, or family members, and are less likely to become obsolete because of changing trends or gender norms.”⁠
⁠
This relates to being more thoughtful with your clothing, to think more critically about what one puts on the body, where it came from, and what your relationship with the garment may be. “Sustainable fashion is about creating clothing that people genuinely connect with and want to wear again and again,” they said. “By focusing on timeless designs, inclusive fits, and messages that resonate across different communities, we're aiming to create pieces that stay in wardrobes longer and ultimately reduce unnecessary consumption.”⁠
⁠
Sustainability is as about taking a holistic and intersectional approach to the items in your life, to reduce harm to people, planet, and animals 🌱 If you’re interested in exploring more gender neutral and unisex clothing from brands like Plant Faced, visit the link for a full list ↑⁠
⁠
#Sustainability #GenderNeutral #LGBT #SustainableFashion #Fashion

posts.caption:
The FIFA World Cup fever is in full swing, which means lots of brands are pushing a lot of merch. But is any of it sustainable? How would you even know? ⚽️⁠
⁠
Our suggestion is to start with cute vintage jerseys 🤭 But, as far as new items for the 2026 games, consider how a brand rates. If you can't find a brand? Reconsider. as unlabelled clothing is less transparent clothing, meaning it's harder (or impossible) to know anything about its supply chain and production methods.⁠
⁠
Also? Be mindful of slightly greenwashed buzzwords around merch, as textile-to-textile recycling is having a moment. It's not all that it seems though 🤨⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about sustainability and the World Cup 🏟️ How do you approach sports merch? Share in the comments, then learn about second hand styles at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#WorldCup #WorldCup2026 #Football #Soccer #VintageJersey
